title: MsgDecodeError.code typed as loose string instead of JxlErrorCode, losing exhaustive error handling at the worker boundary
description: MsgDecodeError.code is declared as `string` (line 140) rather than `JxlErrorCode`. Callers that receive this message and switch/dispatch on the code field get no type-narrowing, making it easy to silently miss new error codes or mishandle existing ones. The same problem exists on MsgEncodeError (line 278). The JxlErrorCode union is already exported from errors.ts and is the obvious correct type for both fields.

title: MsgEncodeError.code typed as loose string instead of JxlErrorCode
description: Same problem as errors-1 for the encode path. MsgEncodeError.code is `string` (line 278), not `JxlErrorCode`. Any code that pattern-matches on this field to construct a JxlError or display a user-facing message loses the compile-time guarantee that only known codes are produced.

title: partialPixelStride is documented as required when partialPixels is present but the type allows it to be absent
description: The JSDoc comment on line 144 states 'required when partialPixels is present', yet the field is declared `partialPixelStride?: number` — optional. Any consumer that reads partialPixels without checking partialPixelStride will silently use an incorrect stride (or undefined), producing corrupt pixel data. A discriminated-union or a conditional type would enforce this invariant at compile time.
code_excerpt:
partialPixels?: ArrayBuffer;       // transferred; present for TruncatedStream
  partialInfo?: ImageInfo;
  partialPixelStride?: number;       // bytes per row of partialPixels; required when partialPixels is present
  partialStage?: DecodeStage;        // stage at which the error occurred; present when partialPixels is present

title: JxlError declares and manually assigns cause after already passing it to super(), creating a redundant write and a readonly-in-base conflict
description: Line 30 passes `{ cause: opts.cause }` to `super()`, which sets `Error.prototype.cause` via the standard ES2022 mechanism. Line 35 then re-assigns `this.cause` via the class's own `readonly cause?: unknown` declaration. The class-level redeclaration shadows the base property, which means serializers/inspectors that walk the prototype chain may observe two `cause` bindings. While not a runtime crash, it is an unnecessary double-write and adds confusion for downstream error handling code that inspects cause chains.

title: DecodeOptions.budgetMs accepts zero and negative values with no type-level guard
description: budgetMs is declared as `number` (line 80) with no lower-bound constraint. A caller passing 0 or a negative value would create a session that immediately budget-exceeds on the first push, producing a confusing BudgetExceeded error with no decoded pixels. The type should be `number & { __brand: 'positive' }` or at minimum a JSDoc note; runtime callers cannot rely on type-only enforcement but a comment + downstream clamp would prevent silent misuse.

title: EncodeOptions.effort is constrained to 1-9 but allowExpertOptions can unlock effort=11 with no corresponding type widening
description: effort is typed as `1|2|3|4|5|6|7|8|9` (line 160), but the JSDoc on allowExpertOptions (line 298-301) documents that effort=11 is valid when allowExpertOptions=true. A caller that sets effort=11 gets a TypeScript compile error even when allowExpertOptions is set. This forces callers to use a cast (`as any`) which then silently bypasses all type checking. The effort field should be widened to include 10 and 11, or the type should use an overloaded pattern conditioned on allowExpertOptions.

category: no structured error taxonomy at wire boundary
title: No typed helper to construct MsgDecodeError / MsgEncodeError from JxlError, risking code/message mismatches across implementations
description: Worker implementations independently construct MsgDecodeError and MsgEncodeError literals. There is no factory or guard function (e.g. `toDecodeErrorMsg(err: JxlError, sessionId: string): MsgDecodeError`) that centralises mapping from a JxlError to the wire message. Each worker implementation must manually set code, message, and optional fields correctly, with no compile-time check that the code is a valid JxlErrorCode. A small factory in errors.ts or protocol.ts would eliminate this class of drift.

title: DecodeSession and EncodeSession use Promise rejection for all errors with no typed Result discriminant
description: All session errors surface exclusively through promise rejection (done(), push(), close()). There is no `Result<T, JxlError>` type or discriminated-union return to allow callers to handle errors without try/catch blocks. This forces every async consumer to wrap in try/catch and manually test `err instanceof JxlError` before accessing err.code, increasing the chance that callers swallow or mis-categorise errors. An exported `type JxlResult<T> = { ok: true; value: T } | { ok: false; error: JxlError }` would be an additive, non-breaking improvement.

title: frameIndexing accepts any string with only a comment-documented regex; no exported validator guard
description: The JSDoc on frameIndexing (line 291-295) documents the exact validation regex `^(0*|1[01]*)$` and the constraint that if any character is '0' all must be '0'. However this validation is entirely in prose — no exported `isValidFrameIndexing(s: string): boolean` or branded type exists. Worker or session implementations that fail to replicate this regex exactly will silently produce invalid codestreams. An exported validator in types.ts would centralise the constraint and allow callers to provide early feedback.
